mission.title: Install and Configure Cubefs on Kubernetes
mission.description: CubeFS is an open-source cloud-native distributed file and object storage system designed for scalability and performance. Installing CubeFS allows you to leverage a robust storage solution for your Kubernetes applications.

steps.title: Add the CubeFS Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the CubeFS Helm repository to your local Helm setup to access the CubeFS charts. Run the following command:
```bash
helm repo add cubefs https://charts.cubefs.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install CubeFS using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install CubeFS in a new namespace called 'cubefs'. This command will deploy CubeFS with the specified version:
```bash
helm install cubefs cubefs/cubefs --version 3.5.3 --namespace cubefs --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the CubeFS pods are running successfully. Use the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace cubefs
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure that your CubeFS deployment has appropriate resource limits, edit the deployment configuration. You can do this by running:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment cubefs --namespace cubefs
```
In the editor, add resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "2Gi"
    cpu: "1"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
To access the CubeFS service, set up port forwarding to your local machine. Run the following command:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/cubefs --namespace cubefs 17010:17010
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of CubeFS will show all pods in the 'cubefs' namespace as running. You will also be able to access the CubeFS service through the port forwarding setup.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add cubefs https://charts.cubefs.io
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the CubeFS deployment, use the following command to uninstall the Helm release. This will remove all associated resources created by the release.
```bash
helm uninstall cubefs --namespace cubefs
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
After uninstalling the Helm release, you may need to manually delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd cubefsclusters.cubefs.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace cubefs
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, remove the 'cubefs' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been deleted by checking the list of namespaces.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace cubefs
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of your CubeFS deployment. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace cubefs -o yaml > cubefs-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the CubeFS Helm repository and then run the upgrade command to update to the latest version. Replace '3.6.0' with the desired version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade cubefs cubefs/cubefs --version 3.6.0 --namespace cubefs
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, verify that the CubeFS pods are running the new version successfully. Use the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace cubefs
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your CubeFS pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs of the pods to diagnose the issue. Use the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace cubefs
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you are unable to access the CubeFS service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. Verify the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace cubefs
```
If the service is not running, check the deployment status.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits set for the CubeFS deployment. Use the following command to view the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get deployment cubefs --namespace cubefs -o yaml
```
Adjust the resource limits as necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Persistent volume claim pending
troubleshooting.description:
If your PVCs are stuck in a pending state, check if there are available persistent volumes that match the PVC requirements. Use the following command to check the PVC status:
```bash
kubectl get pvc --namespace cubefs
```
If no suitable PVs are available, you may need to create them or adjust the PVC specifications.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
